title: Threat Hunting Foothold

headings.text: Scenario 1: Brute-Forcing via SSH on Jumphost
Scenario 2: Remote Code Execution on Web01
Scenario 2: Abuse of Built-In System Tools (LOLBAS)
Scenario 1: Command and Control over DNS
Scenario 2: Command and Control over Cloud Applications
Scenario 3: Command and Control over Encrypted HTTP Traffic

overview: This section focuses on how to detect early intrusion attempts (initial foothold) and the subsequent execution phase once adversaries breach defenses. It outlines the attacker’s mindset, various tactics to gain initial access, and methods to execute malicious commands using built-in tools or scripting.

study.overview: This section focuses on how to detect early intrusion attempts (initial foothold) and the subsequent execution phase once adversaries breach defenses. It outlines the attacker’s mindset, various tactics to gain initial access, and methods to execute malicious commands using built-in tools or scripting.
study.whySoc: Use this note as an analyst reference: understand the concept, identify the relevant telemetry, pivot through evidence, and document the decision path clearly.
keyConcepts: Scenario 1: Brute-Forcing via SSH on Jumphost
Scenario 2: Remote Code Execution on Web01
workflow: Identify the user, host, process, network flow, or identity object involved.
Collect the highest-value logs first and preserve timestamps and assumptions.
Pivot through related notes, telemetry sources, and MITRE references.
Document findings, gaps, containment options, and detection improvements.
tips: The foothold isn’t limited to workstations—it extends to any entry point (account or machine) that an attacker can leverage.
Note: The key idea is that while adversaries try to hide, their actions still leave traces in logs and process metadata that can be hunted.
